coredata.dc:title: The combined effect of green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots blended jackfruit seed biodiesel and acetylene gas tested on the dual fuel engine
coredata.dc:creator: Gavaskar, T.
Ramanan M, Venkata
Arun, K.
Arivazhagan, S.
coredata.dc:description: The main goal of this research is to make a single-cylinder, dual-fuel diesel engine use less fossil fuel and produce less pollution. As fuel sources, acetylene gas as an injected fuel, 100% premium diesel, biodiesel from jackfruit seeds, and 50 parts per million of novel green nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots prepared from citrus juice and studied for their properties were chosen. In this study, cylinder pressure, heat release rate, brake thermal efficiency, brake-specific fuel consumption, exhaust gas temperature, hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide, oxides of nitrogen, and smoke emissions were analysed. At 100% load, the fuel blend (20% jackfruit seed biodiesel + 80% premium diesel + 50 parts per million of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives by biological route + 6 liquids per minute of acetylene gas) performed better than 100% premium diesel in terms of brake-specific fuel consumption (8.24%), exhaust gas temperature (5.88%), and oxides of nitrogen (13.29%). Then this fuel blend had a lower rate of brake thermal efficiency (5.14%), hydrocarbon content (23.36%), carbon monoxide (23.92%), smoke emission (14.72%), cylinder pressure (3.73%), and heat release rate (3.778%) when compared with the 100% premium diesel at 100% load.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The main goal of this research is to make a single-cylinder, dual-fuel diesel engine use less fossil fuel and produce less pollution. As fuel sources, acetylene gas as an injected fuel, 100% premium diesel, biodiesel from jackfruit seeds, and 50 parts per million of novel green nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots prepared from citrus juice and studied for their properties were chosen. In this study, cylinder pressure, heat release rate, brake thermal efficiency, brake-specific fuel consumption, exhaust gas temperature, hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide, oxides of nitrogen, and smoke emissions were analysed. At 100% load, the fuel blend (20% jackfruit seed biodiesel + 80% premium diesel + 50 parts per million of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives by biological route + 6 liquids per minute of acetylene gas) performed better than 100% premium diesel in terms of brake-specific fuel consumption (8.24%), exhaust gas temperature (5.88%), and oxides of nitrogen (13.29%). Then this fuel blend had a lower rate of brake thermal efficiency (5.14%), hydrocarbon content (23.36%), carbon monoxide (23.92%), smoke emission (14.72%), cylinder pressure (3.73%), and heat release rate (3.778%) when compared with the 100% premium diesel at 100% load.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In the current situation, it is hard for the auto industry to control the pollution that comes from engines that run on petroleum. There are some major pollutants produced by internal combustion engines, such as carbon monoxide, nitric oxide, hydrocarbons, and smoke emissions. Concerning the control of these pollutants from the engine, several methodologies have been validated in the past decades by various researchers [1,2]. To control such harmful pollutants, several researchers used various alternative fuels for internal combustion engines. Researchers are being experimental with several alternative fuels such as alcohols, vegetable oils, bio-diesel, acetylene, hydrogen, LPG, and CNG in an engine [3,4]. Additionally, biodiesels and biofuels are extracted from biomasses and wastes such as plastic, municipal solid waste, waste gases, used cooking oils, etc. [5]. It is highly effective and is used to safeguard the national energy security in an environmentally friendly manner by including conventional sources of energy and minimizing dependency on imported, depleting fossil fuels to meet the energy needs of the country's urban and huge rural populations [4]. Amidst all other expressed alternative fuels, the global researcher identified that vegetable oil, acetylene, and hydrogen appear to be a progressive resolution to the fast depletion of conventional petroleum fuels, which transform into a sustainable energy source with non-biodegradable properties [6,7].

The several types of biodiesels acquired from waste, non-utilized, or leftover seeds (biomass) can be additionally utilized as an effective fuel for a diesel engine, which diminishes the fossil fuel consumption, cost, and energy demanded all over the world [8]. Then the utilization of jackfruit seed oil was elected for this current research work, which was collected from the leftover jackfruit seeds at no cost [9,10]. Moreover, the immediate consumption of pure waste seed oils in an internal combustion engine without any refinement leads to inferior performance because of their very high viscosity when compared to petroleum fuels [11]. Therefore, the transesterification process of raw jackfruit seed oil was accomplished to reduce the high viscosity utilized in the engine as biodiesel. Although the consumption of biodiesel only reduces CO and HC emissions, there will be a huge increase in smoke intensity [12]. According to recent research articles, to decrease the very high smoke intensity, acetylene fuel is prescribed to be used directly in the engine by initiating it into the intake manifold. Because acetylene has higher-level combustible properties, which help in the combustion process and discharge very few pollutants from the exhaust emissions, Acetylene gas is a renewable, non-toxic, and biodegradable alternative fuel, which, on combustion in the engine, will produce only water as a product [4,13,14]. It has a very high calorific value, which causes more energy release during combustion, i.e., burning, which might increase the performance of the engine. By utilizing Jackfruit biodiesel and acetylene as an alternative fuel in the engine, several research analyses were conducted by various researchers, who also documented their observations from the acquired results [15,16]. According to the research that has been conducted, acetylene gas has excellent combustion characteristics that are comparable to those of hydrogen. Because of the engine's faster flame speed and wider flammability limit, it is possible to operate the engine with a very lean combination of air and acetylene.

In addition, a catalyst is not required for the reaction that takes place in the gaseous phase. Gas-phase processes, in contrast to those using liquid fuels, are naturally homogenous. They may quickly combine without the need for any components in the correct ratios, and even the management of combustion by-products is a little bit simpler.

Dhanaraju et al. (2016) [17] studied 1-hexanol in mahua seed oil biodiesel blends for diesel engines. A compression-ignition engine was fed 10%, 20%, and 30% MSOME with diesel. The engine's performance and emissions were compared to diesel's baseline statistics. The BTE for MSOME blends was higher, while BSFC, HC, and smoke opacity were lower than diesel fuel. This may be because oxygenated, high-cetane fuels burn more efficiently. All blends have similar NOx values relative to diesel. Adding 1-hexanol (an ignition improver) in volume ratios of 0.5% and 1% to MSOME30 to evaluate engine performance and emissions improves engine combustion and minimizes pollutants. The M30 brake mix has a thermal efficiency of 33.49%. M30 mix gasoline for CI engines reduces smoke density by 19.72%, CO emissions by 14.28%, and NOx emissions somewhat. M30 fuel has a 0.76% lower BSFC than diesel. Unburned hydrocarbon emissions were 12.06% lower than diesel. M30 reduced CO emissions by 14.28% more than diesel. M30 has lower NOx emissions than diesel. The M30's NOx emissions were 2.10% lower than the diesel's. 10-millilitres of 1-hexanol improved brake thermal efficiency and BSFC the most. The brakes had a 34.57% thermal efficiency.

Swamy et al. (2019) [18] studied the influence of 1-butanol on tamarind biodiesel diesel engine characteristics. This study examines engine characteristics by adding butanol to tamarind biodiesel at 5%, 10%, and 15% by volume. When 5% butanol was added to the biodiesel mix, the brakes became 3.21% more thermally efficient than the standard value. Compared to tamarind biodiesel, carbon monoxide, hydrocarbon, and smoke opacity emissions were all 7.25, 6.52, and 6.2% lower at peak load. Nitrogen oxide emissions increased somewhat at all loads. Maximum in-cylinder pressure occurs 7–10° after TDC for the investigated fuels, while TSME 20 with 5% butanol has a higher value of 67.98 bars. At peak load, 75.85 J/degree crank angle (CA) is 2° before TDC for 5% butanol in the TSME 20 mix. The HRR for the TSME 20 mix with more butanol was lower. Adding oxygenate to the tamarind biodiesel mix might be a sustainable fuel source.

These advantages are considerable, and they have the potential to assist diesel engines in overcoming challenges such as wall dampness and inadequate lubrication [19]. Even at high compression ratios, very few researchers have been able to run acetylene in modified CI engines in dual fuel mode because of its high thermal diffusivity and its ability to create a homogenous charge when combined with air.

Reddy et al. (2020) [20] studied the effect of diethyl ether on a diesel engine using waste mango seed biodiesel. Preliminary experiments were done on the Kirloskar diesel engine, utilizing diesel and mango seed biodiesel blends MSME10, MSME20, MSME30, and MSME40 tested alongside diesel. MSME20's performance indicators improved. MSME20 was mixed with 5%, 10%, or 15% diethyl ether by volume. According to testing, adding 5% DEE to MSME20 improved brake thermal efficiency by 3.96% at full load. MSME20 DEE-5 has a higher HRR of 75.75 J/° CA and an in-cylinder pressure of 67.82 bars than other ternary fuel mixes. Compared to full load, CO, HC, and smoke emissions were decreased by 11.2%, 10.41%, and 9.58% for the MSME20 DEE 5%. MSME-20 DEE5% biodiesel may help diesel engines achieve the above-mentioned benefits. Based on the studies, a mixture of MSME 20 DEE and 5% biodiesel could be used as an alternative fuel in diesel engines. This would lead to better performance and less pollution, helping to save energy and protect the environment.

Traditional methods include reacting calcium carbide with water to make acetylene from calcium carbide, which is a by-product of calcium carbonate [21]. However, in modern times, acetylene may also be made from natural gas, which is a naturally renewable source. It is a colourless gas that smells like garlic and has no visible form.

Dhanaraju et al. (2022) [22] tested a diesel engine running on lemon peel biodiesel mixtures. Lemon peel oil was blended with diesel fuel to make a 10%, 20%, and 30% fuel mixture. All mixes were tested in diesel-like conditions. The 20% lemon peel oil blend increased performance and reduced emissions, according to testing. Diethyl ether was also mixed with 20% lemon peel oil in two volumes, 5% and 10%. Diethyl ether added to LPO20 boosted brake thermal efficiency by 3.7% at peak load. HC, CO, NOx, and smoke have been decreased by 24.4%, 16.9%, 11.8%, and 12.5%, respectively, at full load. The LPO20 with 10% DEE has shown promising engine characteristics. The DIESEL-RK modelling indicated slightly greater BTE, cylinder pressure, and NOx emissions for the LPO 20 DEE 10 under full load than the experimental data. The results of the experiments were compared to the modelled outcomes. Theoretical and practical testing showed that adding 10% DEE to LPO 20 improved engine performance and lowered emissions. Diesel-RK modelling shows a minimal rise in BTE, CP, and NOx at maximum load for the LPO 20 DEE-10 engine.

Dhanaraju et al. (2020, p. 23) tested the effect of oxygenated additions on a diesel engine using waste tamarind biodiesel. In this study, diethyl ether, dimethyl ether, and dimethyl carbonate interact with tamarind seed methyl ester (TSME-20) biodiesel blends at variable proportions (6% and 12%). The study examines engine properties. The addition of 12% diethyl ether to TSME-20 improved the brakes' thermal efficiency. This was 4.2% better than the tamarind biodiesel mix. TSME20 DEE 12 reduces undesirable tailpipe emissions such as carbon monoxide, hydrocarbons, oxides of nitrogen, and smoke opacity compared to diesel fuel at full load. The decreases are 10.68%, 33.3%, 10.33%, and 27.72%. The DIESEL-RK simulation results are compared with experimental values obtained under identical operating circumstances. The addition of 12% diethyl ether to TSME 20 has shown encouraging experimental and theoretical engine characteristics. After validating engine modelling software data with experimental values for optimum fuels, it was discovered that TSME-20 DEE-12% biodiesel had improved engine characteristics in both techniques. When simulation results and real-world data match up well, it is possible to trust numerical forecasts.

Acetylene can be ignited in the same way that hydrogen can, but it is a lot safer than hydrogen. Acetylene could be used as an alternative fuel because it burns well, has a wide range of flammability, and has a faster flame speed. In addition to this, the adiabatic flame temperature of acetylene is greater than that of hydrogen, and the minimum ignition energy of a combination of acetylene and air is the lowest of all of the flammable gases that are currently on the market. On the other hand, acetylene is notoriously hard to burn because it has a higher temperature at which it starts to burn on its own. Because of this, many people who work on engines have tried using acetylene in diesel engines when they are operating in dual fuel mode. In addition, diesel engines had problems with the walls getting wet, but this was fixed by using acetylene gas because it mixes better with air. Acetylene gas can easily be sucked into the combustion chamber, even in diesel engines with higher compression ratios. Using electronic injectors, researchers injected the acetylene at three different rates: 110 g/s, 180 g/s, and 240 g/s. Researchers Lakshmanan and Nagarajan used a dual fuel combustion strategy, injected acetylene at flow rates of 0.20 kg/h, 0.26 kg/h, and 0.39 kg/h [24], and investigated the effects of altering injection procedures on the performance characteristics of acetylene.

Nanomaterials are vital for nanoscience and nanotechnology [25,26]. Size, shape, and surface morphology govern nanoscale materials physicochemical, chemical, optical, and electrical characteristics [27,28]. Nanomaterials characteristics are determined during synthesis [4,29]. Green chemistry should be considered while making and using non-materials [30]. The three primary phases in nanomaterial synthesis that should be considered from a green chemistry viewpoint are the choice of solvent medium, the choice of an ecologically benign precursor, and the choice of a nontoxic material for stabilization and functionalization [31]. This thesis discusses the synthesis, characterization, and application of carbon quantum dots and their nanocomposites [32]. The Green technique for synthesising carbon-based nanomaterials (CBN) and heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) uses citrus juice as a precursor and the hydrothermal process. Green heteroatom-doped CQDs are employed for fluorescent metal ion detection, antioxidants, and bio-imaging [33]. The detailed literature survey for the present research work was tabulated in Table 1 .

Under the dual-fuel mode of a diesel engine, the addition of acetylene exhibits an ample effect on its performance behaviour. When acetylene-rich air was burned in a diesel engine that ran on biodiesel, the results were measured. When acetylene is added to fuel blends, there is a huge drop in CO emissions and a huge improvement in performance. A direct installation of acetylene in the binary fuel mode exhibits an extreme effect on smoke and NOx emissions [34]. A replacement of alcohol with acetylene reveals a common one that was experimented with by other authors. The introduction of acetylene with alcohol injection in the consumption stage improves the performance of the engine and decreases the NOx emissions, whereas there is a significant reduction in CO and HC emissions. While increasing the absorption of acetylene in the air, it also increases the NOx emission and decreases the smoke density in the diesel engine [35]. Additionally, there is a consequential decrease in CO and HC with the acetylene double-fuel operating engine [32,36]. On the other hand, the energy portion of acetylene in the diesel engine increases BTE under extensive conditions, though there is a striking decrease in BTE identified with an extreme rise in acetylene energy division in the case of dual-fuel mode [37].

From all the above research, it was concluded that limited research has been allowed on the usage of acetylene as an alternative fuel in a compression ignition engine's dual fuel mode, with the goal of reducing exhaust emissions. Therefore, the current research aspires to decrease the exhaust emissions from the diesel engine manipulated with jackfruit seed biodiesel and acetylene in a dual fuel mode.

The jackfruit, scientifically known as Artocarpus heterophyllus Lam., is a type of tree that may be used for a variety of purposes, including as a source of food, lumber, fuel, fodder, medicinal items, and industrial items. Trees play an important role in the conservation of the environment because they are so successful at preventing soil erosion and improving the quality of the land they grow on. Jackfruit is classified as a commercial fruit, despite the fact that it is virtually never farmed in conventional plantations and is instead often discovered in the backyards of homes. This evergreen tropical tree is indigenous to India, which is its country of origin. Numerous tropical nations, including Malaysia, Myanmar, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Brazil, the West Indies, and Pakistan, are major producers of this crop.

Jack fruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus) is a popular seasonal fruit that is grown in the western ghats of South India and in the forests of Malaysia. It is part of the kingdom Plantae, the phylum Magnoliophyta, the class Magnoliopsida, the order Rosales, the genus Artocarpus, the species Artocarpus heterophyllus, and the class Magnoliopsida. As compared to the other nations on the globe, India produces the most jackfruit (1436 metric tons), making it the leader. After three days of drying, jackfruit seeds have a fat content of 7.39%, a fibre content of 13.27%, a protein content of 15.26%, a starch content of 12.78%, and a water content of 9.68%. When eating jackfruit in India, the seeds are often thrown away after the fruit has been consumed. The mature jackfruit has yellow flesh that has the taste of bananas and is shaped like bulbs. Inside of these “bulbs” are the jackfruit's seeds. The shape of the seeds may range from round to oval, ellipsoidal, or oblong, depending on the particular variety. In general, the seeds are between two and 3 cm in length and one to one and a half centimetres in diameter. They have a coloration of light brown. The moisture content of the seed comes in at around 2.78%. The seeds contain a significant amount of protein due to their composition. The percentage of protein that may be found in jackfruit seeds is 20.19%. The protein levels are greater than those found in foods derived from high-protein animal sources such as beef and marine seafood. The carbohydrate content of jackfruit seeds is much higher than that of other types of seeds. These seeds have a carbohydrate content that is 51.82% of their total weight. Three kilograms of wasted jackfruit seeds were used to produce 1 L of raw jackfruit seed oil for biodiesel production.

Jackfruit is cultivated commercially in over 32 countries throughout the globe, with the majority of its cultivation taking place in Southeast Asian nations. In addition to India, Sri Lanka is another country that cultivates it. China, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Papua New Guinea are the countries included in this list. India is the world's second-biggest producer of jackfruit, with an annual output of 1.83 million metric tons harvested on 0.16 million hectares of land. Both the southern and northern regions of India have a significant population of these animals. The southern Indian states of Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu; the eastern Indian states of Assam and Tripura; the northern Indian states of Bihar and Uttar Pradesh; and the foothills of the Himalaya all have significant jackfruit growing zones.

Because of its cheap price and exceptional flavour, the jackfruit is sometimes referred to as the “poor man's fruit” in India. It is possible that each fruit contains anywhere between 100 and 500 seeds. The seed coat is made up of a thin, waxy, parchment-like testa (husk) and a tegmen that is brownish in colour and made of membranous tissue. In most cases, the cotyledons are not of comparable size, and the endosperm is only present in trace amounts. After that, the oil extracted from jackfruit seeds was selected to be used as a source of biodiesel. The amount of oil that makes up the jackfruit seed may range anywhere from 40 to 45% of the total seed mass. In the course of their work, several of the researchers were extracting hydrogen gas, acetylene gas, and many other types of gas from various sources of waste. As a supplemental source of fuel, acetylene gas was used in the study that was carried out. Higher viscosity, lower heating value, and slow ignition are the primary challenges presented by jackfruit seed biodiesel.

Then, in order to improve the efficiency of the engine and reduce the amount of pollution that was released into the environment, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives were synthesized using a biological route. These nanoadditives had a high energetic value and a higher surface-to-volume ratio. Because of this, the jackfruit seed biodiesel was combined with premium diesel fuel, acetylene gas, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives produced via a biological process, and acetylene gas. Then, these test fuel blends were made, and all of the physical and chemical qualities were analysed in accordance with the ASTM fuel requirements. The results showed that their properties were very comparable to those of premium diesel fuel.

In fact, there is no published technical literature on the characteristics of performance, combustion, and pollutant emissions driven by this kind of fuel mix concentration for diesel engine applications. The number of studies that were conducted to determine the optimal blending concentration of green nanoadditives in the base fuel in order to maximize diesel engine performance while minimizing pollution emissions was very low. In addition, the improved qualities of jackfruit seed biodiesel, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives produced via a biological process, and premium diesel modified using acetylene gas induction were also demonstrated. Rather than relying more and more on fossil fuels, fuel mixes like these may make use of renewable alternatives to traditional fuel sources like biodiesel and nanoadditives.

This can assist in easing growing environmental concerns brought on by rising pollution levels. Blend A consisted of 100% premium diesel. Blend B included 20% Jackfruit seed biodiesel plus 80% premium diesel and 50 ppm nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as nanoadditives by the biological route) plus A6 LPM (6 LPM of acetylene gas. Then the Blend C included 20% Jackfruit seed biodiesel +80% Premium Diesel +50 ppm (nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives by biological route) + A5 LPM (5 LPM of Acetylene Gas), while Blend D had 20% Jackfruit seed biodiesel + 80% Premium Diesel + 50 ppm (nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives by biological route) + A3 LPM (3 LPM of Acetylene Gas). Blend D is prepared with 20% Jackfruit seed biodiesel, 80% premium diesel, and 50-ppm nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives by biological route). The purpose of these experiments was to determine the influence of green nanoadditives in jackfruit seed biodiesel and premium diesel with acetylene gas induction at different concentrations in order to assess the characteristics of dual-fuel diesel engines without making any modifications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:list-item.ce:label: (a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were made with lemon juice as the source of carbon, and a single-step hydrothermal synthesis method was used to make the CQDs. The overall process of producing green synthesized carbon quantum dots from raw citrus lemon juice is displayed in Fig. 1 . In a typical method, 20 mL of filtered lemon juice were placed in a muffle furnace with a 100-mL autoclave made of Teflon-lined stainless steel and heated to a temperature of 200° Celsius for 3 h. Following that step, the temperature of the reaction mixture was allowed to return to normal. After dissolving the black paste that had been produced in 15 mL of water, it was centrifuged at a speed of 3000 revolutions per minute for 15 min to separate the soluble material from the insoluble material. After adding dichloromethane to the brown reaction mixture, it was then centrifuged for 20 min at a speed of three thousand revolutions per minute to separate the unreacted organic moieties [38]. A brownish-yellowish supernatant was eventually produced after separating the aqueous layer from the lower organic layer and centrifuging it at 12,000 rpm for 20 min three times to eliminate larger particles. The reaction mixture was separated using column chromatography with silica gel as the stationary phase and dichloromethane as the solvent. This allowed for the production of CQDs with a lower particle size distribution and a narrower range of particle sizes. The CQD solution that was produced consequently was stored in the refrigerator at a temperature of 4 °C for characterization and other uses [39].

To make nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), 20 mL of filtered fresh lemon juice and 2 mL of ethylenediamine co-reagent were put in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The green synthesis of NCQDs were displayed in Fig. 2 . The autoclave was then placed in a furnace at a temperature of 200° Celsius for 3 h. After that, it was allowed to cool at ambient temperature, and the newly formed black paste was dissolved in 15 mL of water before being centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. This was done to remove any insoluble, unreacted material. After adding dichloromethane to the brown solution that had been generated, the mixture was centrifuged at a speed of 3000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate any organic moieties that had not yet reacted. In this brown solution, the earlier synthesized carbon quantum dots were mixed at an equivalent ratio. The brownish-yellowish supernatant was obtained after separating the aqueous layer from the lower organic layer and centrifuging it at a speed of 12,000 rpm for 20 min, three times. This process was done to eliminate larger-sized particles. The brown solution was separated by column chromatography using silica gel as the stationary phase and dichloromethane as the solvent. This was done to get NCQDs with a smaller particle size distribution and a narrower range of particle sizes. The solution containing the NCQDs that were produced consequently was placed in a refrigerator and kept at a temperature of 4 °C for characterization and other uses [18].
(b)
Characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

When using FTIR, one of its benefits is that it can collect the whole spectrum over the whole frequency range at once, with a consistent level of resolution across the whole range. For this investigation, FTIR spectra were collected using a SHIMADZU-IR Prestige-2 spectrometer and were collected across the range of 400 to 4000 cm−1. In this study, a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Scientific) was used to find out the photoluminescent characteristics of the samples. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) is the capacity to make thin (0.5–1.5 μm) optical sections through fluorescent specimens that have a thickness that ranges from up to 50 μm. These sections may be produced to examine fluorescent molecules. Confocal microscopy with argon and helium lasers was used to get the pictures of the cells using a Zeiss LSM 510 Meta instrument. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) has been the method of choice because of its atomic-level resolution, which leads to direct visual information on size, shape, dispersion, and structure. In this study, TEM with a model called Jeol/JEM 2100 and a source called LaB6 with a resolution point of 0.23 nm and a voltage of 200 kV was performed.

After they were made, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were looked at with a transmission electron microscope to check their shape and size. According to the findings, CQDs are nanoparticles that have a quasi-spherical shape and a restricted size distribution [24]. The particle size of the as-generated CQDs is dispersed in a diameter range of 2–10 nm, with an average of 5.8 nm based on a statistical analysis of more than 90 dots. This was found by using the software Image J to figure out the results from a TEM picture. The TEM image of carbon quantum dots was displayed in Fig. 3 A and Fig. B.

An examination using the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was carried out to explain the functional groups that are located on the surface of CQDs. As can be observed in the spectrum in Fig. 4 , there is a wide, strong band at 3202 cm−1 that may be attributed to the stretching vibration of the carboxylic O–H bonds. A vibration of the C–O bond may be responsible for the band at 1053 cm−1. The presence of bands at 2938 cm−1 indicates the presence of an aliphatic C–H bond, while the emergence of bands in the range of 1669–1053 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of C–O functional groups. The existence of COO-vibrational stretch may be determined from the peaks with the characteristic energies of 1594 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1. Additionally, the presence of typical stretching vibrations for CC in aromatic hydrocarbons at 1293 cm−1 verifies the presence of the aromatic skeleton in the produced CQDs [12,14–16]. Because of this, the FTIR analysis demonstrates the presence of significant functional groups on the surface of the CQDs that were produced from lemon juice [40]. The high water solubility of CQDs is because they have many functional groups on their surface [17–19].

Before they put on the glass slide, the CQDs had to be diluted in the water solution. This was done so that the CQDs could be individually distributed for fluorescence imaging under single-dot conditions [23]. Under fluorescence microscopy, it was possible to easily identify the specimen's fluorescence, and the brilliant fluorescent pictures that were obtained confirmed the specimen's uniform dispersion of fluorescent CQDs were displayed in Fig. 5 A and Fig. B.

The bio-imaging potential of the CQDs was investigated after treating MCF7 cells with a solution containing 0.025 mg/mL of CQDs generated from lemon juice. After being incubated for 4 h, the MCF7 cells were washed to get rid of any CQDs that got outside the cells. The cells were then analysed using fluorescence microscopy with laser excitations of 405, 488, and 561 nm. We discovered that the tagged cells were lighted with multicolour pictures owing to fluorescence originating from the CQDs dispersed throughout the cell which was illustrated in Fig. 6 A and Fig. B, and they displayed blue, green, and red fluorescence corresponding to the laser excitation at 405 nm, 488 nm, and 561 nm. According to the results, carbon quantum dots made from lemon juice that are very water-soluble, photo stable, and fluorescent could work very well as a fluorescence imaging probe [41].

An entire Fig. 7 A, Fig. 7B and Fig. 7C reveal several sample TEM images of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). According to the findings, the NCQDs are almost spherical nanoparticles that are dispersed in an excellently uniform manner and have a restricted size distribution. Based on the statistical analysis of more than 90 dots, the Gaussian-fitting curve indicates that the particle size of the as-prepared CDs has a diameter range of 1–6 nm, with an average of 3 nm. This information was obtained from the distribution of the particle size [42].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns show a broad and intense diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 230 and a weak peak at 2θ = 420. These peaks are assigned to the (002) and (101) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon, as shown in Fig. 8 . This indicates the amorphous nature of the NCQDs and is based on a previous structure analysis of disordered amorphous graphitic carbon quantum.

The technique of FTIR was used to get information on the surface functional group of NCQDs. There is a large overlap of strong bands in the range of 3070–3600 cm−1, and this range may be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the O–H and N–H bonds [43]. This can be observed in the spectrum Fig. 9 , and these bands overlap. The vibrational band of the C–O bond, which is located at 1112 cm−1, shows the presence of primary OH groups on the surface of NCQDs. The observation of bands at a frequency of 2934 cm−1 and in the range of 1040–1300 cm−1 is evidence that aliphatic C–H and C–N/C–O functional groups are present. The existence of an amino functional group may be inferred from the presence of a sharp band at 1546 cm−1, which can be ascribed to an N–H vibration and a deformation band. Additionally, the presence of characteristic stretching and bending vibrational bands for C–H as well as stretching vibrations for CC in aromatic hydrocarbons at 3060, 770, and 1434 cm−1 confirms the presence of the aromatic skeleton (sp2 hybridized carbon) in the prepared NCQDs. These bands can be found in aromatic hydrocarbons. Because of this, the FTIR analysis reveals that lemon juice-derived NCQDs include functional groups on the surface, such as carbonyls, alcohols, amines, aromatic rings, and carbonyls, in addition to sp2/sp3 core carbon. The presence of a variety of functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs is responsible for the great water solubility shown by these nanoparticles [44].

To acquire more evidence on the NCQDs' chemical composition, 1H NMR spectroscopy was carried out, as shown in Fig. 10 . In 1H NMR spectra, a chemical shift (δ) of 2–3 ppm indicates that a proton (α) is bound to nitrogen, while a ppm reading in the range of 3–4 ppm indicates that a proton is bound to oxygen. The chemical shift (δ) of 9–7 ppm reveals the aromatic or sp2 protons, whereas the chemical shift (δ) of 2.5–1.0 ppm shows the protons neighbouring the aromatic carbon or sp2 protons. The presence of protons that are connected to hydroxyl or carbonyl groups is indicated by a shift of 7–4.0 ppm, as described in Refs. [5,45]. The findings from FTIR and 1H NMR show that the functional groups of NCQDs are consistent with each other [46].

The confocal microscopy photos of MCF7 cells that were taken after they had been treated with NCQDs can be seen in Figs. 11 a, b, c and d. The vitality of the cells can be seen in the bright field picture that was produced following the incubation process. After being excited at 405, 488, and 561 nm, respectively, bright blue, green, and red luminescence was seen in the cytoplasm. This showed that NCQDs had a high cell membrane permeability [47]. This was found after the excitation wavelengths were determined to be 405 nm, 488 nm, and 561 nm. The results suggest that carbon quantum dots made from lemon juice that are highly water-soluble, stable under light, and fluorescent could be used as a good fluorescence imaging probe [48].

For this research, the required quantity of Jackfruit seed biodiesel, acetylene gas, and premium diesel was purchased from local oil and gas retailers in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, and India. The used test fuel blends and their detailed descriptions are displayed in Table 2 . These test fuel blends were prepared by magnetic stirring for about 35 min, and the ultra-sonication process was conducted for 10–15 min, correspondingly. Then the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives were blended with biodiesel to produce the required test fuel blends and were investigated with a stability test, which results in there being no settling of nanoadditives up to 98 h. Then the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoadditives were synthesized and characterized successfully. The test fuel properties of fuel blends were tabulated in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure of the research work

ce:list-item.ce:label: (a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Diesel engine setup and its instruments

ce:section.ce:para: For this research work, a 3.7 kW Kirloskar diesel engine with a naturally aspirated single cylinder diesel engine and a water-cooled system was chosen, and the technical specification of the diesel engine was displayed in Table 4 and its layout was illustrated in Fig. 12 . Initially, the eddy current dynamometer was installed with a single-cylinder diesel engine to add the external load to the engine as 0 A–16 A. The crank angle was checked and determined by an electronic encoder that had a photo-emitter device with sensors fitted to the engine flywheel. The test fuel and airflow were monitored by the U-tube manometer, which had a graduated burette and orifice meter. Then the solenoid gas injector, along with the non-return valve, was placed above the inlet valve of the diesel engine cylinder to provide enough acetylene gas to establish the dual fuel engine. By using the proximity sensor device, the opening and closing of the diesel engine were sensed and controlled by an electronic control unit with a received voltage signal.

The supply of acetylene gas to the diesel engine was conducted by various systems, like a control valve, flow meter, pressure regulator, high-pressure acetylene gas cylinder, gas flow meter, flash arrestor, and flame gas trap, correspondingly. Among these systems, both the control valve and pressure regulator instrument were connected to the acetylene gas cylinder, which supplies the gas to the diesel engine with regulated pressure, and the flow rate was monitored by a gas flow meter. The flame arrestor and flame gas trap were installed to avoid the backfire of the acetylene gas while running the diesel engine. For the measurement of tailpipe emissions of HC, CO, and NOx, the AVL 444 Di-gas analyser and AVL 437C smoke meter were fixed to the diesel engine.
(b)
Acetylene gas supply to the diesel engine

The Acetylene gas with a 99.9% pure gas cylinder was procured from the local gas supplier and was used for this research at a pressure of 170 bar for the usage of Acetylene gas supply in dual fuel mode. Then the normal diesel engine fuel intake manifold was altered by installing an induction nozzle for the supply of acetylene gas to the engine and to perform as a dual fuel engine. With the assistance of a Bronkhorst digital gas flow meter, the flow rate of acetylene gas to the dual fuel engine was monitored and measured successfully, with the highest gas flow rate of 16 LPM. To eliminate the backfire or misfire, the flame arrestor was linked with the engine and acetylene gas cylinder.
(c)
Uncertainty analysis of the research work

Errors and uncertainty in this research came from many different places, such as environmental issues, instrument calibration, visualization, instrument choice, tests, and installations. Through this analysis, the accuracy and analytical measurements were estimated and verified with the standard uncertainty analysis [49]. The Holman method was used to conduct an uncertainty analysis of the experimental results and errors. The used instruments and their uncertainty, range, and accuracy displayed in Table 5 , and the gross percentage of uncertainty was discussed in Equation (1):

ce:list-item.ce:label: (a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Performance analysis of the dual fuel engine

ce:section.ce:para: Among all of the diesel engine's performance characteristics, the brake thermal efficiency (BTE) test is the most important. It is defined as the combination of the indicated thermal efficiency rate and the mechanical energy efficiency rate [25,50]. Fig. 13 display the distinction between brake thermal efficiency and brake power of the engine concerning entire test fuel blends compared with mineral diesel fuel. In comparison with mineral diesel, the entire mixture had a lower range of BTE at the entire load condition. At peak load, it was observed that the test fuel blends had a BTE range of A (31.48%), B (29.94%), C (27.40%), D (26.25%) and E (25.31%) respectively. Among these fuel blends, fuel blend B had a 5.14% minimal range of BTE compared with mineral diesel at higher load conditions. In addition, the rest of the fuel blends (C, D, and E) possess a lowered rate of BTE; this could be owing to a lack of combustion, a peak rate of viscosity, an inferior air supply, or the low volatility nature of the fuel blends. The test fuel blend (B) had a closer range of BTE with mineral diesel at the entire load condition due to the supply of acetylene gas and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives [51]. The minimal reduction of BTE was recorded for blend B compared with mineral diesel at all load conditions and was attained by optimal LPM of acetylene gas, nanoadditives, and biodiesel fuel presence, enhanced combustion of waste cooking biodiesel by optimal air intake, and higher flame velocity of acetylene gas [52]. The Blend B has a BTE rate that was reduced by 5.14% when compared to premium diesel fuel operating under greater loads. Because Blend B had a BTE that was 8.48%, 12.32%, and 15.47% greater than the other test fuel blends (Blend C, D, and E), this fuel blend has a BTE that is equivalent to that of baseline premium diesel. Blend B was tested under peak load conditions. This trend was achieved thanks to enhanced mixing, improved combustible mixture formation, and increased combustion efficiency. Other things that contributed to this trend were the effects of 50 ppm nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives and 20% Jackfruit seed biodiesel, which led to the best use of acetylene energy (6 LPM) at optimum load.

The brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) was used to estimate the total energy intake or consumption from the fuel to the shaft power, which was gained from the diesel engine. If the engine's cylinder wall temperature were calculated equivalently, its total energy consumption would improve the total power output of the given fuel blends [53]. Fig. 14 display the distinction of brake-specific fuel consumption versus brake power of the engine for entire test fuel blends compared with mineral diesel fuel. At higher load conditions, mineral diesel (0.18 kg/kW/h) had a lowered range of BSFC rate, and the rest of the fuel blends deserve a higher BSFC at lower loads and a lowered rate of BSFC at higher load conditions with an increased range [45]. The peak rate of BSFC was recorded for blend E (0.2811 kg/kW/h) at higher brake power owing to the minimal rate of calorific value resulting in a higher rate of fuel consumption, peak mass flow rate, and greater viscosity and deduced heating value of the biodiesel. By supplying acetylene gas at 6 LPM and 50 ppm of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives blended with biodiesel to the diesel engine, the BSFC rate was deduced to be 0.2 kg/kW/h, 0.232 kg/kW/h, and 0.25 kg/kW/h for Blend B, C, and D at peak load conditions. This could be caused by the addition of acetylene gas, better fuel and air mixture formation, complete combustion of the test fuel blends, and the peak flame speed of acetylene gas [54].

The BSFC rate for Blend B was 8.24% greater than that of premium diesel fuel when it was subjected to higher loads. When compared with the other test fuel blends (Blend C, D, and E), Blend B exhibited a lower rate of BSFC under peak load conditions of 10.58%, 23.39%, and 35.211%; hence, this test fuel blend has a somewhat greater range than the baseline premium diesel. The greater flame velocity of acetylene gas at 6 LPM causes quick combustion, which suppresses ignition centres and causes BSFC to rise. This trend was accomplished as a result of an increased flow rate of acetylene gas. However, under moderate to high loading circumstances, acetylene shows an efficient use of input energy. This results in a reduction of BSEC at higher loads, which ultimately results in an increase in the thermal efficiency of the dual-fuel engine at optimal load. Also looked at were the effects of adding nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanoparticles and jackfruit seed biodiesel, both of which improved the way the fuel burned.

In general, EGT delivered from diesel engines results in a quantity of heat release at the time of combustion in the engine cylinder for some duration, and this EGT has more sway over the formation of pollutant exhaust emissions. It also gives a clear assessment of the air and fuel ratios of the fuel blends, diesel engine performance rate, quantity of oxygen content, and HRR during the combustion process [55,56]. Fig. 15 show the discrepancy of EGT versus brake power of the engine concerning entire test fuel blends compared with mineral diesel fuel. From the plot, it was observed that based on the engine brake power, the EGT for entire fuel blends was increased, and at peak load, a higher rate of EGT was recorded. Then, at peak load, the premium diesel (310 °C) had a lowered rate of EGT, and the rest of the fuel blends had a higher rate of EGT. By the influence of acetylene gas and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, the blends B (321 °C), C (372 °C), D (395 °C), and E (418 °C) had a higher rate of EGT at peak load conditions [57]. Which was attained by the deduced rate of fuel combustion, peak viscosity of the biodiesel, higher quantity of fuel burned after burning and controlled combustion phase, rapid rate of combustion, supply of acetylene and nanoadditives, higher thermal energy supply, and a higher rate of HRR [58].

When running at a higher load, Blend B had an EGT rate that was 5.88% higher than premium diesel fuel. When compared with the other test fuel blends (Mix C, D, and E), Blend B had a lower rate of EGT under peak load conditions by 7.20%, 11.55%, and 22.63%, respectively; hence, this fuel blend has a greater range than baseline premium diesel. This trend was accomplished because of an increased flow rate of acetylene gas, an increase in combustion temperature at enhanced load, fast combustion induced by higher flame velocities of acetylene gas at 6 LPM suppressing ignition centres, and increased combustion temperature. It is because of the larger heat transfer that is induced by the conduction of gaseous molecules at the enhanced flow rate that ultimately results in a reduction in the thermal efficiency that was stated before. Again, the enhanced BTE is reflected in the increased EGT at 3 and 5 LPM in contrast to that at 6 LPM. This improvement may be attributable to the efficient usage of the heat that is emitted as well as to steady combustion. The reduction in EGT that occurs in a dual-fuel engine will result in an improvement in the longevity of the engine components, thereby boosting the dependability of customized engines in comparison to standard diesel engines. Furthermore, the impact of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives with an efficient catalyst nature and jackfruit seed biodiesel offering a higher oxygen supply for better combustion, both of which enhance combustion properties, was investigated.
(b)
Pollutant emission of the dual fuel engine

Generally, the unburned rate and partial combustion of the test fuel blends were identified and estimated by hydrocarbon emissions (HC), and when we fuel biodiesel fuel blends to the diesel engine, it results in a minimal quantity of HC emissions owing to a better O2 level and cetane number followed by the complete combustion of the fuel [58]. Fig. 16 display the difference in HC emissions and engine brake power for all test fuel blends when compared to mineral diesel fuel. From the plot, it was noticed that at peak load, the premium diesel had a higher rate of HC emissions compared with the rest of the fuel blends at 62.3 ppm. Next, the Jackfruit biodiesel, acetylene gas, and nanoadditives blended fuel blends have a very low rate of HC emissions compared to premium diesel fuel at peak load conditions: Blend B (51.8 ppm), Blend C (44.2 ppm), Blend D (35.2 ppm), and Blend E (28.1 ppm). Among these results, the Blend D and E had a lowered rate of HC emission due to a minimal rate of ID, better oxygen supply and combustion rate, a peak rate of cetane number, an optimal fuel-air ratio, acetylene addition, a rapid rate of burning, and complete combustion [15].

Blend B cut HC emissions by 23.36% compared to premium diesel fuel when the load was higher. Compared to premium diesel, blends C, D, and E cut hydrocarbon emissions at peak load by 56.191%, 114.42%, and 204.186%, respectively. This trend was achieved by increasing acetylene gas flow rate, combustion temperature at an improved load, quick combustion generated by faster flame velocities of acetylene gas at 6 LPM, suppressing ignition centres, and increasing combustion temperature. Because of the greater heat transfer caused by the conduction of gaseous molecules at a higher flow rate, thermal efficiency is reduced. Again, the higher BTE is reflected in the reduced HC emissions at 3 and 5 LPM compared to 6 LPM. This improvement may be due to the effective use of heat released and stable combustion. The decrease in hydrocarbon emissions generated by a dual-fuel engine will enhance the engine's lifetime, which will increase its dependability compared to a regular diesel engine. Additionally, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and jackfruit seed biodiesel have increased combustion capabilities. Because of the induction of acetylene at all loading parameters other than full load. When the engine is pushed to handle larger loads, it might attain its peak cylinder pressure and temperature, resulting in complete combustion. This leads to higher BTE and lower carbon-based emissions. Because the flame speed of the mixture went up to 1.5 m per second, the combustion properties got even better. This made hydrocarbon emissions go down a lot when acetylene was burned with a dual fuel.

The lack of oxygen in the fuel contributed to the formation of CO emissions, which will help turn the carbon molecules into carbon dioxide. In this reaction, hydrocarbon molecules did not combust to a greater extent, so the formation of carbon monoxide emissions occurred. Besides, CO emissions also happen due to poor fuel mixture, local rich zones, and incomplete combustion of the fuel [5]. Fig. 17 exhibit the dissimilarity of CO emission versus brake power of the engine concerning entire fuel blends compared with premium diesel fuel. In this plot, the CO emissions for all the test fuel blends increased with increased load conditions.

Blend B cuts CO emissions by 23.80% compared to premium diesel fuel when running with a heavier load. In contrast to premium diesel, Blends C, D, and E reduced CO emissions by 18.42%, 77.96%, and 214.51%, respectively, at peak load. Since the CO emissions are lower when burning the mixture with more acetylene, it is safe to assume that the charge is uniform and that the upgraded engine is burning a lean mixture. The quantity of CO released is considerably reduced when acetylene is induced. In addition, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and jackfruit seed biodiesel increased combustion quality. The induction of acetylene happens at all loading parameters other than full load since the engine may reach its peak cylinder pressure and temperature when subjected to greater loads, resulting in complete combustion. This happens because the engine can reach its potential. This increases BTE while also reducing CO emissions. The combustion characteristics were further enhanced by increasing the flame velocity of the mixture to 1.5 m/s. This resulted in a considerable decrease in CO emissions when acetylene was burned with a dual fuel. Because of the greater combustion temperature and improved efficiency of the combustion process with increasing load compared to neat premium diesel fuel. This trend was achieved by better combustion of the fuel blends, a higher oxygen supply rate, acetylene gas addition to the fuel blends, the presence of nanoadditives resulting in a higher surface area to volume ratio of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, and a higher OH presence in the fuel blends. The acetylene gas and atmospheric air molecules reacted in the diesel engine to offer better oxygen supply, an air-fuel mixture, and combustion, leading to reduced CO emissions [18].

Oxides of nitrogen emissions were formed by peak and higher air ratios in the diesel engine, which predominantly depended on in-cylinder temperature, oxygen content in the fuel blends, and fuel and air interactions [46,59]. Fig. 18 display the contrast between oxides of nitrogen emissions and the brake power of the engine for entire fuel blends compared with premium diesel fuel. From the NOx emission plot, it was perceived that, based on the engine load conditions, the NOx emission of the test fuel blends was increased, and at peak load, entire fuel blends had a higher rate of NOx emission. Next, the premium diesel (600 ppm) had an extremely low rate of NOx emission compared to the rest of the fuel blends at peak load. Then the rest of the fuel blends B, C, D, and E had 670 ppm, 775 ppm, 807 ppm, and 948 ppm at peak load, which were noted to have a higher rate of NOx emission compared with premium diesel fuel. This could be owing to the greater oxygen supply of the Jackfruit biodiesel, inferior combustion, improved cylinder temperature, and higher latent heat of fuel vaporization. With the supply of acetylene gas and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, the NOx emission was improved to a greater extent for blends B, C, and D at peak load conditions due to the quantity of atmospheric air, the peak rate of calorific value, the higher cycle temperature inside the cylinder, and the higher thermal energy supply at the time of combustion [60].

The Blend B achieved higher NOx emissions than premium diesel fuel, even while the engine was under heavier loads. Blend B had higher NOx emissions, at 11.66% greater than those of premium diesel. At peak load, blends C, D, and E had higher NOx emissions by 22.58%, 25.63%, and 36.70% relative to premium diesel, respectively. It is conceivable that the extra load requires more gasoline. This caused the reaction temperature and cylinder peak pressure to rise, which increased NOx levels. Because of acetylene, NOx generation rises sharply from intermediate to peak loading levels. NOx emissions rise when the flow rate of acetylene gas, biodiesel, and nanoadditives in fuel blends is increased. This may be owing to the faster energy release, as seen by the higher peak cycle pressure in the pressure-crank angle graph. If so, it may be connected to the increase in peak cycle temperature. This may be due to a quicker flame velocity and greater combustion temperatures with a higher HRR, as explained in the section on combustion. According to the Zeldovich mechanism, high reaction temperatures, an adequate reaction time, and an abundance of oxygen lead to NOx formation in the combustion chamber. Overall, an increase in NOx is found when mass flow rate is increased. A quicker and less regulated combustion release leads to a rise in peak cylinder pressure, as shown in a diagram of pressure vs. crank angle. Lean mixtures may reduce NOx emissions in an acetylene engine. The sparse mixture lowers the temperature, which slows the chemical reaction and reduces NOx's kinetic energy.

Smoke emissions originated in a diesel engine with a minimal fuel and air ratio; at higher engine load conditions, there was excess fuel supply in the combustion chamber, excess pressure, and higher fuel-rich zones [61]. Fig. 19 exhibit the contrast of smoke emissions versus brake power of the engine concerning entire fuel blends compared with mineral diesel fuel. At the entire load condition, premium diesel fuel recorded higher smoke emissions, whereas the rest of the blends had a lower range of smoke emissions. In the peak load condition, premium diesel had a higher range of smoke emissions at 52.3%, and the rest of the fuel blends B, C, D, and E had 47.2%, 39.5%, 34.8%, and 24.9%, respectively. Expect the premium diesel fuel; the rest of the fuel blends possess a lowered range of smoke emission owing to better combustion by enriched oxygen, which was offered by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, and a higher surface area per volume, which will oxidize the fuel-air mixture easily, which could be the reason for minimal smoke emissions. Then, the presence of acetylene gas in the fuel blends produces a significant reduction in the smoke emission through the intake of atmospheric air supply, better combustion of unburned soot particles, and post-secondary oxidation in the combustion chamber [24].

Even though the diesel engine was running under heavier loads, Blend B was still able to produce a lower level of smoke emissions in comparison to premium diesel fuel. This was the case even when Blend B was used. When compared to premium gasoline, it was found that there was a 10.80% point reduction in smoke emissions. When compared to premium diesel, blends C, D, and E reduced smoke emissions by 32.40%, 50.28%, and 110.04%, respectively, when operating at peak load. This decrease was due to the blends' ability to burn more completely. This was in contrast to the premium diesel's decrease in smoke emission decrease. It is possible that this is due to the increased energy that is generated during the premixed combustion phase, in addition to the higher diffusivity of gaseous molecules that promote homogeneous mixing in dual fuel mode. The decrease in smoke emissions may be attributed to instantaneous combustion, which leads to a reduction in the diffusion combustion stage and, consequently, prevents acetylene from being subjected to the pyrolysis stage. In general, the proportion of smoke levels tightly depends on the fuel flow rate, injection time, and mixture composition necessary for optimal combustion. Other factors, such as the effects of biodiesel fuel and nanoadditives included in the fuel blends.
(c)
Combustion characteristics of the dual fuel engine

In-cylinder pressure is the pressure that builds up in the engine cylinder during the power stroke of a cycle. This pressure is equal to the work done by the tested fuel burn rate. Additionally, the fuel-air mixture, ignition delay time, combustion of fuels at the premixed combustion stage, atomization, viscosity, evaporation, and thermal energy [5,45] all had a significant impact on this in-cylinder pressure. Fig. 20 parade how the pressure in the cylinders and the crank angle of the engine are different for whole fuel blends and mineral diesel fuel. From the plot, it was observed that mineral diesel fuel (54.8 bar) had a higher range of in-cylinder pressure compared with the rest of the fuel blends. Next, the fuel blends B, C, D, and E had a lowered range of cylinder pressure rise at higher load conditions related to mineral diesel fuel. Then the test fuel blends B, C, and D had 52 bars, 51.2 bars, and 50 bars of pressure, respectively. It was noticed that the fuel blends B and C had a nearer range with mineral diesel fuel owing to the presence of nanoadditives in the biodiesel fuel, acetylene gas induction, higher premixed combustion burning, a longer ignition delay period, dual fuel operation, and the higher energy release and combustion rate of acetylene gas and biodiesel fuel.

At higher load conditions, Blend B had lower cylinder pressure than premium diesel. Following that, the cylinder pressure of Blend B dropped by 5.38% when compared to premium diesel fuel. Compared to premium diesel, Blends C and D decreased cylinder pressure loss by 7.03 and 9.6%, respectively, at peak load conditions. Quick combustion produces more heat, which in a dual-fuel engine raises cylinder pressure. When gas is given at lower loads, the fuel mixture tends to be rich, resulting in incomplete combustion. Part of the fuel is not consumed, causing emissions concerns at lower loads. When the load is increased to an appropriate level (80%), combustion is greatly boosted, allowing for an effective gaseous fuel-air combination. This occurs when the load is ideal. At this point, cylinder pressure may continue to climb due to a higher rate of heat release, and performance may improve, as the charge in the combustion chamber is now being entirely combusted, unlike when it was under partial load. When the flow rate of acetylene, biodiesel, and nanoadditives was increased, the combustion chamber pressure rose quickly. This was owing to the increased heat created during premixed combustion. During combustion, a higher acetylene burn rate causes temperatures and pressures inside the cylinder to rise. This may lead to excessively high rates of energy release and a quick rise in pressure. Therefore, the acetylene-air combination is ignited before TDC, wasting fuel energy to offset the compression effort. Dual-fuel engines have less effective work during the power stroke than traditional-fuel engines. The performance of a multi-fuel engine diminishes compared to a premium diesel.

In this research, the heat release rate was calculated using the model (single-zone combustion model), which was used to predict the occurrence of HRR during a combustion cycle (fuel supply from inlet to end of exhaust) [58]. Then the heat transfer rate to the engine cylinder wall

Fig. 21 display the difference of HRR versus crank angle of the engine concerning entire fuel blends compared with mineral diesel fuel. From the plot, it was perceived that mineral diesel fuel had (54.3 J/°CA), which was ranked as having a higher rate of HRR compared to the rest of the fuel blends. Then the test fuel blends B, C, D, and E possess a lowered range of HRR at higher load conditions. However, the fuel blends B, C, and D had a nearer range of HRR related to mineral diesel fuel at peak load conditions. This could be attained by the availability of nanoadditives, a higher ignition delay, and a minimal cetane number, which would result in better combustion of the fuel within the period of ignition delay. Also attained by the usage of a dual-fuel engine with acetylene gas induction, which results in a better-premixed combustion rate, complete and faster combustion of pilot fuel, acetylene gas, and a higher rate of flame speed. Moreover, the fuel blend E had a lower rate of HRR (45 J/°CA) compared with the rest of the fuel blends, owing to minimal premixed combustion, a higher rate of fuel viscosity and density, poor atomization and fuel vaporization, and minimal fuel-air entrainment, all of which produce a higher fuel burning rate in the diffusion phase [26].

With increasing loading, Blend B had a lower HRR than premium diesel. At 1.30% compared to premium diesel fuel, this reduced the HRR of Blend B. Blends C, D, and E reduced HRR by 3.56, 3.14, and 21.51%, respectively, compared to premium diesel at full load. Because of the greater rate of heat release generated by an increase in acetylene flow from 3 to 6 LPM, the HRR is now closer to the TDC. This is due to the fuel that accumulated during the longer wait time. Increasing the flow rate of acetylene to 6 and 8 LPM leads the HRR peak to migrate further from the TDC. A longer ignition delay causes more premixed fuel to be accessible at the beginning of the premixed burning phase. This causes an excessive energy release, which causes a rapid rate of pressure increase. Rapid HRR during acetylene induction raises combustion temperature and engine cylinder pressure, which increases NOx emissions. This phenomenon is described during emission analysis. A few engine professionals have also claimed that greater HRR at an increased flow rate is related to the fast flame propagation speed of the gaseous air mixture. This hypothesis explains why increased HRR occurs at higher flow rates. At a high flow rate, the pre-ignition reaction of the gaseous fuel around the pilot fuel region becomes so active that once the pilot ignites, combustion can take place inside and outside the pilot fuel, and the gaseous fuel can burn so rapidly and almost simultaneously that extremely high energy release rates and a sharp pressure rise result. Because of this, the rate of heat release during acetylene induction indicates a large period of premixed combustion followed by a phase of diffusion combustion that is only slightly less intense. Therefore, one may draw the following conclusion: HRR depends on the ignition delay, the start of the combustion process, the amount of fuel used during uncontrolled combustion, and the difference between the rates of combustion of a pilot and a liquid fuel.

This study uses a dual fuel approach to estimate the effect of jackfruit biodiesel, green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, and acetylene gas on the emissions, combustion, and performance of single-cylinder diesel engines. Its results are as follows:
•
At higher loads, Blend B had a 5.14% lower BTE, but it has a nearer range of BTE related to premium diesel fuel, an 8.24% higher BSFC, and a 5.88% EGT compared with Blend A. Due to the presence of 50 ppm of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as nanoadditives, there is better combustion, sufficient oxygen, and a supply of 6 LPM of acetylene gas to the diesel engine.
•
It was noticed that Blend B achieved a minimal rate of HC of 23.36%, CO of 23.92%, and smoke emission of 14.72% compared to premium diesel fuel at peak load conditions. This was made possible by combining premium diesel fuel with optimal biodiesel, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nanoadditives, and 6 LPM of acetylene gas.
•
At peak load, Blend B had 13.92% more NOx emissions than Premium Diesel. This happened because Blend B had a higher supply of oxygen, a higher rate of combustion, a higher temperature in the cylinder, and a higher latent heat of fuel vaporization.
•
When the Blend B was under a heavier load, the in-cylinder pressure dropped by 3.73% and the HRR was 3.78%. Because of acetylene gas with nanoadditives and the right amount of biodiesel supplied into the dual-fuel engine. This gives the engine better oxygen, a higher calorific value, better combustion, and a higher cetane number.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future studies of the research work

ce:list-item.ce:para: This research project needs a thorough look at nano-emulsions or surfactants so that phase separation can be fixed and the problems with fuel blends' stability can be solved.
Based on the results of tests done with green nanoadditives, changes were made to the nozzle geometry, pilot injection, spray pattern, and swirl ratio of the dual-fuel diesel engine.
During the synthesis process, other waste biomass sources like leaves, shells, and marine algae will be used to make green nanoadditives.
